description_long: This object is a carved stone guard stone featuring a stylized seated human figure with simplified facial features and blocky arms resting on its lap. The figure is integrated into a rectangular base, with the legs and feet roughly indicated, and the back of the object is smoothly curved, suggesting a cloak or drapery. The stone surface is weathered and shows signs of age, with some chipping and surface irregularities, especially along the edges and base. The overall form is monolithic and solid, with no visible joints or separable parts. The color is a pale, off-white to light gray, typical of limestone or marble, and the finish is matte and rough, with little to no reflectivity. This type of object would serve as a protective barrier at the edge of a pathway or entrance, both as a physical obstacle and as a decorative or symbolic marker.
description: A weathered, pale stone guard stone carved in the form of a seated human figure with a blocky, monolithic shape and rough, matte surface. The object is solid and heavy, with visible signs of age and wear.
description_view_0: Front view shows the stylized face, blocky arms, and legs of the seated figure, with the rectangular base clearly visible.
description_view_1: Left side view highlights the profile of the head, the curvature of the back, and the thickness of the stone base.
description_view_2: Rear view reveals the smooth, curved back and the draped appearance of the upper section, with no facial or limb details.
description_view_3: Right side view mirrors the left, showing the profile of the head and the integration of the figure into the base.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to driveways, historical sites, or along the edge of urban streets to protect corners or mark boundaries, often adjacent to pathways or building entrances.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, standing upright on a flat surface, sometimes partially embedded for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible chipping and rounding along the edges and base, with surface erosion and discoloration likely caused by weather exposure, vehicle contact, and occasional foot traffic. The most affected areas are the corners and lower sections.
